content.text: Python's operators, built-in functions, and language constructs connect to user-defined objects through a set of specially named methods delimited by double underscores —
— it never searches for a plain
attribute. This mechanism, known as Python's

, lets any class participate in operators, iteration, context management, hashing, and dozens of other language protocols simply by defining the right methods. The result is that well-designed domain objects feel native to Python, interoperate with its standard library, and require no bespoke APIs from callers.
in the Python Language Reference — are the hooks through which Python's syntax and built-ins delegate work to user-defined classes. They are characterized by double-underscore prefixes and suffixes:

, and so on. There are approximately 100 such methods defined across Python's data model, organized into protocols: lifecycle, representation, arithmetic, comparison, container, iterator, context management, descriptor, and callable protocols.

(Chapter 3 of the Python Language Reference): a formal specification of the interface every object presents to the interpreter. Unlike Java interfaces or C++ vtables, participation is implicit and duck-typed — a class opts into a protocol by defining the relevant dunder methods, not by extending an abstract base class. This makes the system extremely flexible: you can make any object behave like a number, a container, a file, or a callable by defining the right methods.

Python's expressiveness depends on the data model. Without dunders, every library would invent its own
methods, and its own loop APIs — libraries could not be composed uniformly. NumPy arrays work with
. SQLAlchemy query objects are iterable because they define
. These integrations are free — no adaptation layer required — because all participants speak the same protocol.
For agentic AI development specifically, dunder methods matter when building domain objects:

membership tests, prompt builders that iterate over templates, and LLM clients that act as context managers to guarantee connection cleanup. A class that implements the right dunders fits into standard Python idioms the caller already knows, removing the cognitive load of a bespoke API.

iterations, even a 100 ns overhead per call adds one second. NumPy sidesteps this by implementing dunders in C.

attrs.alt: Dunder method dispatch pipeline: user code → interpreter → MRO search → dunder method. Categories: Lifecycle, Representation, Operators, Container/Protocol. Operator fallback chain and key protocols: Context Manager, Iterator, Descriptor.

A monetary value is the canonical example: it should support addition, comparison, hashing (so it can be stored in a set or as a dict key), and readable display. It should raise
when arithmetic is attempted across different currencies.
Example 1 — Iterator protocol: token sliding window
An LLM pre-processor that slides a fixed-size window over a token list, yielding one chunk per step. Implementing
Example 2 — Context manager: LLM API rate limiter
A context manager that acquires a rate-limit slot before entering the
block and releases it on exit — even if an exception occurs inside the block.

import threading
import time

class RateLimitedSession:
    """
    Wraps an LLM client with a simple token-bucket rate limiter.
    Use as a context manager to guarantee slot release.
    """

    def __init__(self, client, rpm: int = 60):
        self.client   = client
        self.rpm      = rpm
        self._lock    = threading.Semaphore(rpm)
        self._entered = False

    def __enter__(self):
        self._lock.acquire()
        self._entered = True
        self._start   = time.monotonic()
        return self          # bound to the 'as' target

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, tb):
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - self._start
        # ensure at least 60/rpm seconds pass before the slot is released
        gap = (60.0 / self.rpm) - elapsed
        if gap > 0:
            time.sleep(gap)
        self._lock.release()
        self._entered = False
        return False         # do NOT suppress exceptions

    def complete(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        if not self._entered:
            raise RuntimeError("Must use inside 'with' block")
        return self.client.complete(prompt)

# Usage
class FakeClient:
    def complete(self, prompt): return f"response to '{prompt}'"

client = FakeClient()
session = RateLimitedSession(client, rpm=10)
with session as s:
    result = s.complete("hello")
    print(result)

Keep dunders thin. Complex logic belongs in private methods called by the dunder, so the dunder is easy to read and test independently.
on small value objects that will be instantiated millions of times (e.g. token objects, graph nodes) to eliminate per-instance
Each dunder invocation is a type-level attribute lookup through the MRO. CPython caches this lookup in the per-type
slots for built-in types, but for pure-Python classes the lookup is slower — typically 50–100 ns per call. At
iterations, this overhead is significant. NumPy and Pandas sidestep this by implementing dunders in C extension modules.

SQLAlchemy ORM — uses the descriptor protocol (
) extensively for mapped column attributes and relationship loading.
is recommended over rolling your own abstract base:

from collections.abc import MutableMapping

class CaseInsensitiveDict(MutableMapping):
    """
    Inheriting MutableMapping and implementing the 5 abstract methods
    (__getitem__, __setitem__, __delitem__, __iter__, __len__) gives us
    update(), get(), pop(), keys(), values(), items() and == for free.
    """
    def __init__(self): self._data = {}
    def __getitem__(self, key): return self._data[key.lower()]
    def __setitem__(self, key, val): self._data[key.lower()] = val
    def __delitem__(self, key): del self._data[key.lower()]
    def __iter__(self): return iter(self._data)
    def __len__(self): return len(self._data)

d = CaseInsensitiveDict()
d["Content-Type"] = "application/json"
print(d["content-type"])  # application/json
print(len(d))             # 1
print(dict(d))            # {'content-type': 'application/json'}
Dunder methods are not the only way to give objects custom behavior. The alternatives each have different trade-offs:
Objects that integrate with Python syntax, built-ins, and standard library
Implicit invocation; partial protocols cause silent failures
When semantics would be surprising as an operator; public library APIs
Verbose call sites; no built-in interoperability
Type-based dispatch on free functions without modifying the types
No access to self; cannot modify existing classes cleanly
Enforcing interface contracts across unrelated classes
Adds class hierarchy; registration is manual
Structural subtyping checks at type-check time without inheritance
Runtime checks need isinstance(obj, Protocol) — slower
Catches all attribute access — hides bugs; performance cost
